executiveSummary:
## Executive Summary: Medicaid Program Performance Analysis

As of January 2026, the national Medicaid program serves 75.3 million enrollees with total expenditures of $215.9 billion in fiscal year 2024, representing a national average per-enrollee cost of $2,869. Enrollment patterns reflect a significant shift from pandemic-era highs, with most major states experiencing year-over-year declines as continuous coverage protections have ended. California leads with 12.6 million enrollees but posted a notable 6.2% decline, while Michigan experienced the steepest drop at 7.6%. Texas maintains its position as the third-largest program with 4.0 million enrollees and a relatively moderate 3.1% decline, comparable to Pennsylvania's performance. North Carolina stands out as the only major state showing growth at 0.5%, suggesting successful retention strategies worth examining.

Spending efficiency varies dramatically across states, with per-enrollee costs ranging from Florida's $2,031 to Pennsylvania's $4,377, likely reflecting differences in benefit packages, provider payment rates, and population health needs. Texas demonstrates competitive cost management at $2,988 per enrollee, positioned below the national average and significantly lower than comparable large states like New York ($3,431) and Ohio ($3,561). However, quality performance presents opportunities for improvement, with Texas scoring 62 out of 100 and ranking 30th nationally—trailing higher-performing states like Massachusetts (81) and Minnesota (80) but avoiding the bottom tier occupied by southeastern states. Texas's managed care penetration of 93.3% exceeds the national average of 83.2%, indicating strong care coordination infrastructure that could be leveraged to drive quality improvements and cost efficiencies in the coming fiscal year.
texasSpotlight:
## Texas Medicaid Program Performance Analysis

Texas operates the nation's third-largest Medicaid program with 4.03 million enrollees, representing approximately 5.4% of national enrollment despite serving the second-most populous state. The program demonstrates notable cost efficiency with per-enrollee expenditures of $2,988—4.2% above the national average of $2,868 but significantly below peer large states like New York ($3,431) and Pennsylvania ($4,377). Among southern non-expansion states, Texas compares favorably to Florida's lower spending of $2,031 per enrollee, suggesting a more comprehensive benefit structure. The state's 3.1% year-over-year enrollment decline aligns with the national post-pandemic unwinding trend and matches Pennsylvania's decline rate, while performing better than large states like California (-6.2%) and Michigan (-7.6%). Texas's managed care penetration rate of 93.3% substantially exceeds the national average of 83.2%, indicating strong adoption of coordinated care models that may contribute to cost management.

However, quality outcomes present Texas's most significant challenge, with the state ranking 30th nationally with a quality score of 62/100—well below the national average of 65.6 and falling into the "moderate" performance tier. This positions Texas in the bottom half of states and below most large-state peers, though it avoids the bottom-five designation occupied by several southern neighbors like Arkansas (54) and Alabama (52). The quality-cost dynamic reveals a critical area for improvement: while Texas achieves reasonable per-enrollee cost control, the state must focus on translating its high managed care penetration and substantial $12.0 billion total program investment into better health outcomes. Key areas to monitor include whether Texas can leverage its managed care infrastructure to drive quality improvements, how the state navigates ongoing federal oversight activities, and whether current procurement opportunities signal program enhancements that could elevate quality performance while maintaining cost efficiency.

alerts.title: Pennsylvania Per-Enrollee Spending 53% Above National Average
alerts.description: Pennsylvania's per-enrollee spending of $4,377 is significantly higher than the national average of $2,869, indicating potential cost efficiency issues.

alerts.title: Five States Show Severely Low Quality Performance
alerts.description: Arkansas, South Carolina, Alabama, Oklahoma, and Mississippi all scored below 55 on quality measures, well under the national average of 65.6.

alerts.title: Florida Shows Low Spending Despite Large Enrollment
alerts.description: Florida's per-enrollee spending of $2,031 is 29% below the national average despite being the 4th largest Medicaid program by enrollment.

alerts.title: Michigan Enrollment Declined 7.6% Year-Over-Year
alerts.description: Michigan experienced the steepest enrollment decline among major states at -7.6%, potentially indicating access or eligibility issues.

alerts.title: Massachusetts High Spending-High Quality Pattern
alerts.description: Massachusetts combines the highest quality score (81) with high per-enrollee spending ($4,201), suggesting a positive spending-quality correlation.

executiveInsights.title: Texas HHSC quality score significantly below national average
executiveInsights.summary: Texas ranks #30 of 50 states with a quality score of 62/100, well below the national average of 65.6. This places Texas in the 'moderate' tier, closer to low-performing states.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Quality performance directly impacts federal funding, contract renewals, and regulatory scrutiny. Poor quality scores can trigger CMS intervention and affect our contract positioning.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag quality improvement initiatives to HHSC leadership immediately

executiveInsights.title: Texas per-enrollee costs above national average despite enrollment decline
executiveInsights.summary: Texas spends $2,988 per enrollee compared to national average of $2,868, while enrollment dropped 3.1% YoY. Total expenditures remain at $12.04B despite fewer beneficiaries.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Rising per-member costs during enrollment decline suggests cost management challenges that could trigger state budget concerns and contract scrutiny. This creates opportunity for cost optimization consulting.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review cost management strategies with HHSC finance team

executiveInsights.title: Texas managed care penetration exceeds national average by 10+ points
executiveInsights.summary: Texas has 93.3% managed care penetration with 3.86M MC enrollees, significantly above the national average of 83.2%. This represents strong managed care adoption in the state.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: High managed care penetration indicates sophisticated contracting environment and potential for managed care optimization services. This differentiates Texas from other state markets.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review managed care optimization opportunities with account team

executiveInsights.title: New HCBS quality measures for 2028 announced in Federal Register
executiveInsights.summary: CMS announced new Medicaid Home and Community-Based Services Quality Measure Set for 2028. This represents upcoming regulatory changes affecting quality reporting requirements.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: New quality measures will require system updates and compliance preparation, creating potential consulting opportunities. Early preparation gives competitive advantage in supporting HHSC compliance.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag upcoming HCBS quality measure changes to compliance team

executiveInsights.title: Texas procurement portal shows active contracting opportunities
executiveInsights.summary: Texas HHSC Procurement Opportunities portal and Texas ESBD are actively posting procurement opportunities. Multiple procurement channels are being monitored for new RFPs.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Active procurement activity suggests upcoming contract opportunities that Accenture should track for potential bid opportunities beyond the current Medicaid contract.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review current Texas procurement postings for relevant opportunities

riskOpportunity.label: Quality measure gap
riskOpportunity.description: Texas ranks #30 nationally with 62/100 quality score, creating compliance risk and improvement opportunities.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop comprehensive quality improvement roadmap targeting CMS core measures.

riskOpportunity.label: Enrollment decline impact
riskOpportunity.description: Texas Medicaid enrollment dropped 3.1% YoY, potentially reducing contract values and service volumes.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Model contract value scenarios and identify cost optimization opportunities.

riskOpportunity.label: HCBS quality expansion
riskOpportunity.description: New 2028 HCBS quality measure requirements create compliance obligations and service expansion potential.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Position Accenture as lead implementer for HCBS quality measurement systems.

riskOpportunity.label: OIG oversight escalation
riskOpportunity.description: Active HHS OIG Medicaid oversight creates audit risk but also drives demand for compliance solutions.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Strengthen compliance frameworks and offer proactive audit readiness services.

riskOpportunity.label: Managed care dominance
riskOpportunity.description: Texas 93.3% managed care penetration exceeds national average, indicating mature market with efficiency pressures.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop managed care optimization solutions for high-penetration markets.

riskOpportunity.label: HHSC procurement pipeline
riskOpportunity.description: Active Texas HHSC procurement opportunities signal potential new contract vehicles and recompetes.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Monitor ESBD portal daily and prepare capabilities-based responses for emerging RFPs.

riskOpportunity.label: Per-enrollee cost efficiency
riskOpportunity.description: Texas $2,988 per-enrollee cost above national average suggests pressure for cost containment initiatives.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Propose cost reduction initiatives leveraging analytics and automation capabilities.

riskOpportunity.label: Data collection modernization
riskOpportunity.description: New CMS generic information collection requirements drive need for updated data systems and processes.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Position data modernization services to meet evolving CMS reporting requirements.

pulseMetrics.label: Enrollment
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas Medicaid serves 4,032,864 enrollees as of January 2026.

pulseMetrics.label: 12-Mo Enrollment Delta
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas enrollment declined 3.1% year-over-year, tracking close to national unwinding trends.

pulseMetrics.label: Per Enrollee Spend
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas spends $2,988 per enrollee annually, below the national average of $2,869.

pulseMetrics.label: Managed Care Rate
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas managed care penetration of 93.3% exceeds the national average of 83.2%.

pulseMetrics.label: Quality Score
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas quality score of 62 ranks 30th nationally, in the moderate performance tier.

pulseMetrics.label: Active Procurement Items
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Two active Texas procurement portals are being monitored for Medicaid-related opportunities.

pulseMetrics.label: OIG/Regulatory Signals
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Six regulatory and oversight items are being tracked across HHS OIG and Federal Register sources.

pulseMetrics.label: Next Milestone
pulseMetrics.tooltip: No specific upcoming milestones identified in the current data sources.
